coredata.dc:title: Carbon quantum dot coated Fe3O4 hybrid composites for sensitive electrochemical detection of uric acid
coredata.dc:creator: Abbas, Malik Waseem
Soomro, Razium Ali
Kalwar, Nazar Hussain
Zahoor, Mehvish
Avci, Ahmet
Pehlivan, Erol
Hallam, Keith Richard
Willander, Magnus
coredata.dc:description: The study explores carbon quantum dots (C-dots) as potential candidates for enhancing the signal sensitivity of an electrochemical sensor devised for biologically important molecule, such as uric acid (UA). The C-dots were evaluated for their electrochemical characteristics in combination with Fe3O4 nanoparticles (Fe3O4 NPs), which were applied as the primary electro-catalytic promoter. The hybrid nanocomposite (C-dots/Fe3O4 HCs) formation was achieved by facilitating the adsorption of C-dots over Fe3O4 NPs using amine-carbonyl interactions. Unlike, one pot method, the proposed strategy enables aggregation-free coverage of Fe3O4 NPs with highly conductive layer of C-dots that can act as conduction centres to support ultra-fast electron transfer kinetics to satisfy the need of high signal sensitivity. The hybrid composite demonstrated remarkable signal improvement when tested against the electrochemical oxidation of UA. The heighten current response and lower over-potential values enabled development of a DC-amperometric (DC-AMP) sensor for UA with a linear working range of 0.01 to 0.145 μM and signal sensitivity measurable up to 6.0 × 10−9 M. The said improvement was manifested as a synergetic outcome of active redox couple (Fe (III/II)), larger surface area of Fe3O4 NPs engulfed with a layer of highly conductive C-dots acting as efficient charge sensitisers.
coredata.dcterms:subject: C-dots
Magnetite NPs
Uric acid
Electrochemical sensors

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Coupling of carbon quantum dots with Fe3O4 nanoparticles
Electro-kinetic study of the hybrid nanocomposite
Analytical characteristics of the Fe3O4/C-dots based sensors

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Scientific and Technological Research Council of Turkey
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Türkiye Bilimsel ve Teknolojik Araştirma Kurumu

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors would like to acknowledge research support from The Scientific and Technological Research Council of Turkey (TUBITAK) under 2221-Fellowships for Visiting Scientists and Scientists on Sabbatical Leave, the grant number of the project is 1059B211600383 . The authors are also highly thankful to the Technical University, Berlin, Germany and National Centre of Excellence in Analytical Chemistry, University of Sindh, Jamshoro Pakistan for access to material characterisation facilities.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Transmission electron micrographs of: (a) C-dots; (b) Fe3O4 nanoparticles and (c) C-dot/Fe3O4 HCs, with (inset) figure reflecting mishmash of the two nanostructures.
FTIR analysis of (a) C-dots; (b) Fe3O4 NPs; and (c) C-dot/Fe3O4 HCs respectively.
X-ray diffraction pattern recorded for: (a) C-dots; and (b) Fe3O4/C-dots hybrid composite and Fe3O4 NPs.
The typical CV redox peak characteristics of [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− for C-dots/Fe3O4 HCs; Fe3O4 NPs; and C-dots in reference to bare GCE electrodes.
Cyclic voltammetry response recorded for: (a) bare; (b) Fe3O4 NPs; C-dots/Fe3O4 HCs against uric acid (0.01 μM) within the potential window of −1.0 to 1.0 V and scan rate of 50 mVs−1.

(a) DC-amperometric responses for uric acid oxidation in the range of 0.01 to 0.145 μM; with inset figure depicting the response time for sensor (b) corresponding linear fit analysis; and (c) selectivity profile with common interferents, such as AA, DP, and GO.
Analytical comparison of the devised sensor with other electrochemical sensor reported for uric acid.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: ZnO-Au HCs, ZnO nanorods-Au nanoparticles hybrids composite, AuNPs-GO, Au-doped graphene oxide, CL-TiN, chrysanthemum-like titanium nitride, [Ni(phen)2]2+, nickel(II)-bis(1,10-phenanthroline) complex, SWCNT, single-wall carbon nanotubes, MWCNT, multi-wall carbon nanotubes, Pt-Ni Platinum–Nickel Alloy.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Analysis of uric acid in human urine samples using C-dots/Fe3O4 HCs as working electrode.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The values are means of three standard measurements.

head.ce:title: Carbon quantum dot coated Fe3O4 hybrid composites for sensitive electrochemical detection of uric acid

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National Institute for Biotechnology and Genetic Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The study explores carbon quantum dots (C-dots) as potential candidates for enhancing the signal sensitivity of an electrochemical sensor devised for biologically important molecule, such as uric acid (UA). The C-dots were evaluated for their electrochemical characteristics in combination with Fe3O4 nanoparticles (Fe3O4 NPs), which were applied as the primary electro-catalytic promoter. The hybrid nanocomposite (C-dots/Fe3O4 HCs) formation was achieved by facilitating the adsorption of C-dots over Fe3O4 NPs using amine-carbonyl interactions. Unlike, one pot method, the proposed strategy enables aggregation-free coverage of Fe3O4 NPs with highly conductive layer of C-dots that can act as conduction centres to support ultra-fast electron transfer kinetics to satisfy the need of high signal sensitivity. The hybrid composite demonstrated remarkable signal improvement when tested against the electrochemical oxidation of UA. The heighten current response and lower over-potential values enabled development of a DC-amperometric (DC-AMP) sensor for UA with a linear working range of 0.01 to 0.145 μM and signal sensitivity measurable up to 6.0 × 10−9 M. The said improvement was manifested as a synergetic outcome of active redox couple (Fe (III/II)), larger surface area of Fe3O4 NPs engulfed with a layer of highly conductive C-dots acting as efficient charge sensitisers.

ce:section.ce:para: The accurate detection of biological important analytes such as uric acid (UA) is of high importance considering its clinical and toxicological significance. The UA (2,6,8-trihydroxypurine) is one of the basic waste products of nitrogenous base-like purine metabolism in humans [1]. Variations in UA levels are reported to be associated with diseases such as hyperuricemia, gout, Lesch-Nyan disease, obesity, diabetes, high cholesterol, high blood pressure, kidney and heart diseases [2]. Thus, monitoring the concentration of UA in body fluids might facilitate in-time diagnosis of these diseases. The conventional approaches, such as high-performance liquid chromatography, ultraviolet spectrophotometry and mass spectrometry are highly robust and sensitive towards the detection of UA [3–5]. However, the complex multi-step protocols, high sample analysis costs and lab-based practices restrict the wide scope application of these techniques. Unlike these conventional techniques, chemical and enzymatic approaches are much simpler but are considered unreliable due to difficulties such as enzyme stability and chemical interferences [6]. The electrochemical techniques integrated with nanomaterials are promising for on-site detection of such important molecules. This integration of highly versatile nanomaterials with the electrode-based system can drastically enhance the characteristics of sensors, allowing sensitive and reliable quantification of various clinically important biomolecules.

In regard to the electrochemical detection of UA, the direct oxidation using bare glassy carbon electrodes (GCEs) has been widely investigated [7]. However improved sensitivities have only been achieved using nanomaterials as an electrode modifier. The use of single-walled carbon nano-horns for the detection of UA and other biomolecules, such as dopamine and ascorbic acid, has been reported by Zhu and co-workers [8]. Much improved signal sensitivity have been achieved using Fe3O4/graphene composites [9] and, recently, noble metal alloy composites have also been explored for the sensitive detection of UA [10]. In that case, the authors attributed the improved signal response to the synergetic combination of the noble metal and the multi-walled carbon nanotubes. Despite the excellent performance of the nanocomposite modified electrode, the question of achieving high signal sensitivity using cost-effective nanomaterial still exists. This can be addressed by introducing a cheap yet an efficient electro-active transducer, which can drastically improve the electron-transfer process at the surface of electrode, thereby producing measurable electro-catalytic signal even at lower concentrations of the target analyte. In this context, the use of newly-discovered carbon quantum dots (C-dots) as an electrode transducer is gaining significant research attention. Unlike other carbonaceous materials, the C-dots are being recognised for their potential as a new generation material with excellent electron transfer capabilities and as an alternative to other carbonaceous competitors [11]. The C-dots, based on their excellent photoluminance (PL) characteristics, are highly considered in the area of fluorescence-based sensor systems. However, much less is known about their electrochemical characteristics and potential for increasing the signal sensitivity of electrochemical sensors [12].

The C-dots, based on their high surface area and conductivity, can readily facilitate electron-transfer process at the electrode-solution interface, thereby improving the signal response of the redox reaction under investigation. Recently, nitrogen-doped C-dots have shown significant improvement in the electro-catalytic response noted for dopamine detection over GCEs [13]. Moreover, the use of C-dots in combination with multi-walled carbon nanotubes for simultaneous detection of resorcinol, catechol and hydroquinone has also been reported [14].

Despite the wide variety of transition metal oxides available, the use of Fe3O4 nanoparticles (Fe3O4 NPs) in electrode modification is highly encouraged due to their simple redox chemistry, low production cost and minimal environmental impact [15]. Besides this, the bio-compatible nature of Fe3O4 NPs makes them suitable candidates for biosensor development [16]. However, the use of Fe3O4 NPs in electrochemical sensor systems is restricted due to their poor conductivity. This issue has been addressed by approaches where coupling of iron oxide with a variety of carbonaceous materials has shown great improvement [17]. Such coupling can drastically enhance the electrical conductivity, further improving the sensitivity of the developed electrode. A recent attempt of coupling iron oxide with carbon nanofibres has shown significant improvement in the conduction of the hybrid material [18,19]. However, the complexity and high synthesis cost of carbonaceous materials such as graphene and nanotubes increases the overall production cost of the sensor under development [19,20]. Thus, coupling of a carbonaceous material which is both simple to synthesise and possess high conductivity would not only contribute towards enhanced signal sensitivity but could also ensure production of cheap biosensor system. Thus, this work discusses the use of C-dots as potential charge-transfer facilitators when coupled with Fe3O4 NPs. The hybrid material was tested for its capability against electro-catalytic oxidation of UA. The study was carried out in a competitive manner, where the electrochemical characteristics of Fe3O4 NPs, C-dots were evaluated against the C-dots/Fe3O4 HCs nanomaterials. The increased signal response at low over-potential value reflected the significance of C-dots as effective conduction centres responsible for increased electron-transfer kinetics at the electrode surface. Moreover, the successful quantification of UA from human urine samples further justified the workability of the proposed sensor for practical applications.

Analytical grade ferric chloride (FeCl3), ferrous chloride (FeCl2), ammonium hydroxide (NH4OH) 35%, glutaric acid (C₃H₆(COOH)₂), UA, ethanol 25%, Phosphate Buffer Solution (PBS) (pH 7.0) and polyacrylamide (PAM)(C3H5NO)n) were purchased from Sigma Aldrich. Potassium ferricyanide (K₃[Fe(CN)₆]), Potassium Chloride (KCl) were obtained from Merck, Germany. The standard solutions were prepared using Milli-Q water (ρ = 18 MΩ cm). The electrolytic solutions were subjected to nitrogen purge prior to electrochemical measurements.

The C-dots were synthesised using a simple one-step hydrothermal reaction process, with polyacrylamide (PAM) as the primary carbon source. In a typical experiment, 0.1 g of PAM was vortexed in 40 mL of de-ionised water until homogenisation was achieved. The solution was then transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave followed by hydrothermal treatment of 24 h at 270 °C. The reactor was then allowed to cool down to room temperature and the yellow solution was filtered to obtain clear dispersion of C-dots.

The Fe3O4 NPs were prepared using a simple chemical co-precipitation method [21]. Briefly, 1.26 g of FeCl2 and 3.24 g of FeCl3 were solubilised together in the presence of glutaric acid (0.1 g). Chemical precipitation was then achieved by drop-wise addition of NH4OH (1.0 M) until the pH of the solution reached 10. The formation of small black precipitates indicated the completion of reaction. The solution was then maintained at 80 °C for 5 h followed by high speed centrifugation to obtain nano-sized iron oxide. The as-synthesised Fe3O4 NPs were thoroughly washed with de-ionised water and ethanol/acetone to ensure removal of any unattached functionalising agent.

In this method, the glutaric acid was used to ensure size control of the NPs and to induce carbonyl functionality over the surface of Fe3O4 NPs, which would then facilitate their coupling with amine containing C-dots.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Coupling of carbon quantum dots with Fe3O4 nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para: To obtain a hybrid material of C-dots with Fe3O4 NPs, the glutaric acid functionalised Fe3O4 NPs (0.1 mg) were dispersed within 2 mL of pure C-dots. The mixture was allowed to shake overnight followed by centrifugation to collect the hybrid composites. Since the constituents of hybrid material were charged with favourable functionalities, the coupling of the Fe3O4 NPs and C-dots is expected to be facilitated by electro-static interactions, this is further explained in Section 3.1. The hybrid composites are hereon referred as C-dot/Fe3O4 HCs for better readability.

A simple slurry-based method was used to prepare modified GCEs. Before modification, the Fe3O4, C-dots/Fe3O4 HCs nanostructures were converted into suspension form (0.1 g/2 mL ethanol). Three working electrodes were prepared by depositing a 100 μL pre-prepared suspension of C-dots, Fe3O4 NPs and C-dots/Fe3O4 HCs over pre-polished GCEs. The deposited layers were then dried in ambient air conditions followed by coating with Nafion® (2 μL), to restrict the abrasion of deposited materials from the electrode surface. A fixed volume of dispersion (100 μL) used for electrode modification during all the experiments performed using CV, EIS and DC-AMP.

The as-synthesised nanomaterials were characterised using advance analytical techniques such as transmission electron microscopy (TEM; JEOL JSM-7001F), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (ESCA200 spectrometer), Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) (Nicolet 5700 of Thermo), Raman Spectroscopy (Horiba Scientific Jobin Yvon technology) and PL spectroscopy (Perkin Elmer LS-55), X-ray diffraction (XRD; Bruker D-8). The electrochemical studies were performed using an Auto-lab PGSTAT 204 potentiostat/galvanostat (Metrohm) with a three-electrode cell system consisting of a GCE as the working, Ag/AgCl as reference and graphite carbon rod as auxiliary electrode. The cyclic voltammetry (CV) measurements were performed in potential range of −1.0 to 1.0 V with PBS electrolyte (0.1 M) (pH 7.0), scan rate of 50 mVs−1, quiet time, 05 s, and sensitivity 1 × 10−6 (A/V). The K4Fe(CN)6 (0.1 mM) with 0.1 M KCl supporting electrolyte was used to estimate the electro-active surface area of the modified electrodes. The Nyquist plots for the modified electrodes against 0.01 μM of UA were recorded using electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) with frequency range of 10−2 to 10−5 Hz with AC amplitude of 5 mV.

The C-dots and its hybrid composites were initially characterised for their morphological characteristics using TEM. Fig. 1(a) shows the typical TEM image captured for as-synthesised C-dots with evidence of small size and uniform spherical formation. The inset of Fig. 1(a) shows the corresponded size histogram with C-dots size distribution contained within 2–3 nm. The average size of C-dots as estimated from TEM image was 2.5 ± 0.43 nm. The TEM image for Fe3O4 NPs (Fig. 1(b)), is evident of dense population of formed nanoparticle with most of particle acquiring spherical shape. The histogram shown as inset of Fig. 1(b) illustrates the distribution of particle in range of 14–20 nm. The average size of Fe3O4 NPs in this case is estimated to be 17 nm ± 1.2 nm. Fig. 1(c) shows the representative TEM image of hybrid composites (C-dot/Fe3O4 HCs). Interestingly, Fe3O4 NPs can been seen covered by layer of C-dots. Unlike, when using one-pot based approach [22], where formation of hybrid composites usually results in aggregated structures with deformed morphology, this preparation procedure enables C-dots to act as physical barrier which could effectively control inter-particle fusion, thus suppressing the agglomeration of Fe3O4 NPs in suspension. The high-resolution image shown as an inset of Fig. 1(c) is typical of this aggregation-free coverage of Fe3O4 NPs. The size of hybrid composites as estimated from corresponded TEM image was in range of 19–25 ± 1.4 nm.

FTIR analysis was carried to confirm the surface bound interactions between the C-dots and Fe3O4 NPs. Fig. 2 shows the overlay spectra for C-dots, Fe3O4 NPs and C-dots/Fe3O4 HCs. The FTIR spectra for C-dots reveals the existence of amine groups with NH stretching vibrations present at 3400 and 3130 cm−1. The small bands observed at 1630, 1399, 1120 cm−1 are attributed to NH, COCH2 bending vibration, and CN bond of polyacrylamide precursor for C-dot [23]. The FTIR spectra recorded for Fe3O4 NPs depicts the existence of OH vibration of carbonyl and carbonyl stretching of the carboxylate at 2500–3400, 1679 cm−1 respectively. The band observed at 590 cm−1 further confirms the presence of FeO bond in Fe3O4 NPs [24]. The FTIR spectra of C-dots/Fe3O4 HCs (Fig. 2(c)) show slight shifting of band frequencies noted for NH, CO, and CO stretching vibrations indicating the surface bound interaction between the functional moieties of two nanomaterials. In addition, the occurrence of new band around 1400 and 1170 endorses the interaction between FeO bond and the carbonyl moieties of C-dots and Fe3O4 respectively. This confirms the successful co-existence of C-dots and Fe3O4 in the designed hybrid composites.

The formation mechanism of C-dots/Fe3O4 HCs could be explained on the basics of favourable interactive environment that prevails between the surface bound functionalities of C-dots and Fe3O4 NPs. The slight shift in the frequencies of amine and carbonyl groups observed in the FTIR spectra of hybrid composite are evident of the amine-carbonyl interactions that exists between C-dots and its ferrite counterpart. Such interactions provide an indorsing environment where a high density of tiny C-dots can easily reside over the surface of Fe3O4 NPs, permitting the C-dots to suppress the agglomeration between Fe3O4 NPs, concurrent to act as an electron conduction layer to support fast-charge transportation at the interface of the developed electrode. This interaction based coupling is illustrated as schematic diagram in Fig. S1.

Further confirmation and phase identification of Fe3O4 was obtained using Raman spectroscopy. Fig. S2 shows the Raman spectra recorded for C-dots/Fe3O4 HCs with inset figure depicting frequencies noted for Fe3O4 NPs. The Raman spectra consist of peaks centered at 350, 550 and 670 cm−1 representing Eg, T2g, and A1g vibration modes for Fe3O4 NPs [25]. The absence of peak around 1378 cm−1 confirms the formation of Fe3O4 rather than γ-Fe2O3. The spectra recorded for C-dots/Fe3O4 HCs shows additional bands around 1350 and 1602 indicative of D and G-bands associated with sp3 and sp2 hybridized carbon atoms of C-dots. Apparently a small frequency shift was noted for Eg, T2g, and A1g bands in the spectra for C-dots/Fe3O4 HCs indicating the successful coupling of the two nanomaterials. The higher intensity of G band in this case, is evident of C-dots graphitic nature [26].

XPS is considered a powerful tool for determining surface-binding interactions and surface purity in hybrid nanocomposites. The XPS survey spectrum for as-synthesised C-dots is present in Fig. S3. The survey spectrum indicates the presence of peaks attributed to carbon, nitrogen and oxygen. The well-resolved binding energy peaks for O 1s, N 1s and C 1s are positioned at 529.5, 395.9 and 286.5 eV, after charge correction using carbon (1s) for aligning the core-level binding energy. The observed peaks positions are in accordance with papers concerning the formation of C-dots using polyacrylamide as the precursor, confirming their chemical composition [27].

The XPS survey spectrum for the C-dots/Fe3O4 HCs is shown in Fig. S4. The spectrum consists of peaks attributed to iron, carbon, nitrogen and oxygen. The high resolution Fe 2p peaks are shown as inset of Fig. S3. The spectra depicts the presence of two peaks at 723 and 711 eV attributed to Fe 2p½ and Fe 2P 3/2 with no satellite peak at 719 eV. This confirms the as –synthesised material to be Fe3O4 rather than Fe2O3 [28]. Fig. S4(b) shows the Gaussian fitted C1s spectra with three bands resolved for CC, CC/CO and CO positioned at 284.5, 285.7 and 288.7 eV respectively. Whereas, the O1s spectra is shown as Fig. S4(c) with well resolved peaks centred at 530.1, 530.6 and 532.3 eV attributed to OFe, OC and OH respectively [27]. The XPS data analysis further supports the co-existence of C-dots and Fe3O4 NPs in the discussed hybrid composites.

PL studies were carried to evaluate the change in the optical characteristics of C-dots after formation of hybrid composites. Fig. S5 shows the PL spectrum recorded for C-dots and its hybrid counterpart. Unlike, C-dots which were noted to possess intense emission at 567.5 nm, the C-dots/Fe3O4 HCs showed significant intensity decline, which is indicative of the electron-transfer between the two material and thus successful formation of hybrid composites. The inset of the Fig. S5 visualises the decreased fluorescence of C-dots subsequent its hybrid composite formation. The compositional characteristics were further analysed using XRD measurements. Fig. 3 shows the XRD pattern recorded for C-dots and its hybrid counterpart. The XRD pattern (Fig. 3(a)) for C-dots consists of a broad diffraction peak at around 20° 2θ, signifying the amorphous nature of the as-synthesised C-dots. The XRD pattern recorded for the hybrid composite consists of peaks indexed to (220), (311), (400), (422), (511) and (440) planes of pure manganite iron oxide (Fig. 3(b)). The measured pattern was free from peaks indexed to haematite and goethite versions of iron oxide, further justifying the compositional purity of the Fe3O4 nanomaterial. The presence of a broad shoulder around 20° is indicative of the presence of C-dots with Fe3O4 NPs.

The electrochemical characteristics of the modified electrodes were evaluated using K4Fe(CN)6 as redox active probe. Fig. 4 shows the CV profile of the modified electrodes in reference to bare GCE. In each case, the typical redox peak characteristics of [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− is evident. However, the peak shapes, current responses differ significantly. Unlike the bare GCE, the modified electrodes demonstrated significant improvement in measured current response. The current response noted for Fe3O4 NPs modified electrode was three times greater than the bare GCE. In comparison, the C-dot modified electrode showed slightly declined response (2.7 times GCE), which may be the consequence of aggregation occurring from the fusion of C-dots over the surface of GCE. The maximum current response was attained by C-dots/Fe3O4HCs (4.6 times GCE) with peak potentials slightly shifted to lower value. The relatively higher current noted for C-dots/Fe3O4HCs compared to Fe3O4 NPs signifies the capability of C-dots to facilitate ultra-fast electron transfer kinetics and to act as an efficient current conduction centres. The electro-active surface area of the modified electrodes were also estimated from their CV profile recorded against 0.1 mM K4 Fe(CN)6 at different scan rates in range from 50 to 100 mVs−1(Fig. S6). The current v/s scan rate relationship could be described according to Randles-Sevcik equation as:
(1)
Ip = 2.69 ×
10 5
A
D
1 / 2
n
3 / 2
v
1 / 2
C
where, Ip, A, D, n, v and C refer to the recorded current, electrochemical active area (cm2), diffusion co-efficient (6.20 × 10−6 cm2 s−1), number of electrons transferred (n = 1), scan rate and concentration of [Fe(CN) 6]3−/4− (0.1 mM) respectively. As estimated from the slopes of the graphs between current and square root of scan rate (insets of Fig. S6), the electro-active areas were calculated to be 0.78, 0.64 and 1.4 cm2 for Fe3O4 NPs, C-dots and C -dots/Fe3O4HCs respectively. The electro-active area noted for hybrid composite is greater than its Fe3O4 NPs and C-dots counter-part.

To evaluate the potential of C-dots/Fe3O4HCs towards the oxidation of UA, CV measurements were obtained for the modified electrodes against 0.01 μM of UA in PBS (0.1 M) (pH 7.0). Unlike, the bare GCE (Fig. 5(a)) which exhibits low-over potential value of 0.41 and current response of 0.09 μA, the Fe3O4 modified electrode exhibits much satisfactory response with potential value of 0.28 V and current intensity of 10.1 μA (Fig. 5(b)). However, the maximum current increment (35.7 μA) was achieved in the case of C-dots/Fe3O4 HCs, with a large negative potential shift of 0.10 V compared to Fe3O4 NPs.

The C-dots modified GCE exhibited relatively weak response at 0.38 V with current density of 0.618 μA. The current enhancement in the case of C-dots/Fe3O4 HCs is 3.53 and 57.7 times higher than Fe3O4/GCE and C-dot/GCE respectively. The poor response of C-dots in this case is evident of carbon quantum dots incapability to support electro-catalytic oxidation of UA. However, the combination of C-dots in C-dot/Fe3O4 HCs results in an improved electro-catalytic response which is the synergic outcome of active Fe(III)/(II) redox couple of Fe3O4 NPs and high conductivity of C-dots. The schematic diagram present in Fig. S1 represents the general electro-oxidation process that occurs on the surface of C-dot/Fe3O4 HCs. The plausible electro-catalytic mechanism involves the following reactions [29,30]:
(2)
Fe 3
O 4
+ 8
H +
+ 2
e −
→ 3 F
e
2 +
+ 4
H 2
O
(3)
Fe
2 +
→ F
e
3 +
+
e −

At the initial stage, the anodic sweep results in the reduction of Fe (III) to Fe (II) as described by Eqs. (1) and (2). This results in generation of electrons that are facilitated by highly conductive C-dots based charge centres at the electrode interface, enabling large anodic peak current and subsequent oxidation of UA to uric acid 4,5 diol through Fe(III). The Fe (III) at the same instant, de-oxidizes to Fe(II). The cycle continuous and with rise in concentration of UA, a boosted anodic peak response is simultaneously observed.

The performance of the discussed electrode in terms of oxidation potential and current density is much greater than electrodes conventionally-modified with carbon nanotubes, poly(3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene)/reduced graphene oxide, ternary nanocomposite materials and recently reported 3D MnO2 mesh material [31–35].

The increased internal area of the CV loop and higher current density achieved in the case of C-dots/Fe3O4 HCs further intimates that the electrode possesses a relatively higher specific capacitance compared to its pristine counterpart. Therefore, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy was carried out to evaluate the resistance variation of the devised electrodes in the uric acid solution (0.01 μM). Fig. S7 shows the Nyquist plots obtained for C-dots/Fe3O4 HCs and Fe3O4 NPs in reference to bare GCE. Unlike Fe3O4 NPs, where a broad semi-circle is measured in the high frequency region, C-dot/sFe3O4 HCs shows a smaller semi-circle, reflecting its low ion diffusion resistance. The low resistance in this case may be attributed to the surface coverage of C-dots over Fe3O4 responsible for reduction of charge-transfer resistance in the Fe3O4 NPs, subsequently improving the conduction characteristics of the hybrid material.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electro-kinetic study of the hybrid nanocomposite
ce:section.ce:para: To ensure the suitability of the hybrid composites for an electrochemical sensor system, the electro-kinetics of the modified electrodes were construed using chronoamperometric measurements. The chronoamperometry were performed at a fixed potential 0.19 and 0.28 V for C-dots/Fe3O4 HCs and Fe3O4 NPs respectively for UA with concentration range of 0.1 to 0.5 mM in PBS (0.1 M) (pH 7.0). The diffusion co-efficient (D) and rate constants for the devised electrodes were estimated using Cortrell and Galus equations shown as Eqs. (3) and (4) [36]. In this case, C is the concentration of UA, A is the area of the GCE (0.07 cm2) and n, F and π are standards with their usual meanings.

From the chronoamperometric measurement, current versus time (t−1/2) graphs were plotted with linear fit analysis (Fig. S8). The slopes of the corresponding curves were then plotted against the concentration of UA and the final slope values were used to estimate the D value in accordance to Eq. (4) (insets of Fig. S8). The D value for C-dots/Fe3O4 HCs was estimated to be 8.95 × 10−5, which is significantly greater than that estimated for Fe3O4 NPs (1.69 × 10−3) (Fig. S8(b)).
(4)
I = nFAC
D
1 / 2
π
− 1 / 2
t
− 1 / 2

The rate constants were obtained using the Galus equation:
(5)
I c
I L
=
γ
1 / 2
π
1 / 2
=
π
1 / 2
k h
Ct
1 / 2

In this case, Ic and IL are the current response of the modified electrodes in the presence and absence of UA, respectively. kh, is the catalytic constant and C is the concentration of UA, with t representing the elapsed time. Linear fit analysis of the plot of Ic/IL against t1/2 for both electrodes is shown in Fig. S9. The rate constant (k) for C-dot/Fe3O4 HCs was determined to be 1.15 × 104 mol−1 Ls−1. The calculated value is much higher than that noted for pristine Fe3O4 NPs (3.4 × 103 mol−1 Ls−1), proving its feasibility to support higher electron transfer than its C-dots free counterpart. The enhanced kinetics for C-dots/Fe3O4 HCs is an indication that the engulfing Fe3O4 NPs in C-dots layer has a positive catalytic effect on electro-oxidation of UA. The kinetic performance of C-dots/Fe3O4 HCs is better than nano resin modified carbon paste electrode and Nano-ZSM-5nanocomposite material [37,38].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical characteristics of the Fe3O4/C-dots based sensors
ce:section.ce:para:
To obtain maximum signal sensitivity, basic quantification of UA was carried using C-dots/Fe3O4 HCs as working electrode and direct current amperometric (DC-AMP) as primary mode of quantification. The DC-AMP experiments were recorded at room temperature with set potential of (+0.91 V) under continuous stirring conditions. Fig. 6(a) shows the DC-AMP graph (I v/s t) for UA under the concentration range of 0.01 to 0.145 μM with inset figure indicating response time of 4 s. Fig. 6(b) shows the corresponding calibration plot with linear fit analysis. The generated regression equation for this selected region was I
ap = 554.34(CUA) + 98.438 with a correlation coefficient of 0.9817. The I
ap is the anodic peak current and CUA is the concentration of uric acid. The limit of detection and limit of quantification for the developed sensor were estimated to be 6.0 × 10−9 M and 2.0 × 10−8 M, respectively.

The selectivity of the proposed sensor towards common interferents, such as ascorbic acid (AA), dopamine (DP) and glucose (GO) was also recorded. Fig. 6(c) shows the amperometric response for C-dots/Fe3O4 HCs in the presence of these interferents with same concentration as used for UA. It is evident that C-dots/Fe3O4 HCs under a constant potential of +0.19 V is immune to such interferents. This signifies the real-time workability of the proposed sensor system. The repeatability of the discussed electrode was evaluated by measuring the response of five separately modified electrode using the same procedure as mentioned in section (2.5). The variation of current response for the corresponding electrodes against 0.01 μM UA is shown as bar-graph in Fig. S10(a). As seen, the devised electrodes exhibit similar response with minimum variation in the measured current. The stability of the devised electrode was assessed from its capability to produce prominent response towards UA during 30 days of electrode storage in 0.1 M PBS solution at 4 °C. The electrode response was recorded at intervals of 5, 10, 15, 25 and 30 days. The variation in relative current intensity is shown as Fig. S10(b). The trivial variation in the generated current response is evident of the long-term stability of the discussed electrode.

Table 1 compares the analytical characteristics of this sensor with various electrochemical sensor designed for detection of UA. The suitable window of operation and is suggestive of the sensors suitability for clinical analysis of UA.

The practical workability of the proposed sensor was evaluated by monitoring its performance during detecting UA in human urine samples. The urine samples were collected from laboratory individuals against a written consent. Before analysis, the samples were diluted and spiked with known concentrations of standard UA. The samples were then analysed using the DC-AMP mode with C-dots/Fe3O4 HCs as the working electrode. Table 2 shows the acceptable recoveries measured for each urine sample, indicating the practical potential of the proposed sensor in complex matrices such as urine.

In this study, the potential of C-dots has been explored for their use as efficient conduction centres to improve the signal sensitivity of nanomaterial-based electrode sensors. The C-dots were studied in combination with Fe3O4 NPs, where the favourable interaction between the associated moieties resulted in complete coverage of Fe3O4 NPs with C-dots. The as-synthesised C-dot/Fe3O4 HCs possessed aggregation-free morphology with enhanced electro-catalytic characteristics against the electro-chemical oxidation of UA compared to the un-hybrid counterparts. The enhanced current response, high diffusion co-efficient (8.95 × 10−5) and rate constant (1.15 × 104 mol−1 Ls−1) measured for C-dots/Fe3O4 HCs were considered a synergistic outcome of the high surface area of the Fe3O4 NPs, active redox couple (Fe(III/II)) supported by the heightened conductivity of the C-dots. The C-dots/Fe3O4 HCs enabled rapid and selective amperometric determination of UA in the concentration range of 0.01 to 0.145 μM, with signal sensitivity measurable up to 6.0 × 10−9 M. Moreover, the acceptable recoveries noted for spiked UA from human urine samples further validated the assays potential for practical applications.

The authors would like to acknowledge research support from The Scientific and Technological Research Council of Turkey (TUBITAK) under 2221-Fellowships for Visiting Scientists and Scientists on Sabbatical Leave, the grant number of the project is 1059B211600383. The authors are also highly thankful to the Technical University, Berlin, Germany and National Centre of Excellence in Analytical Chemistry, University of Sindh, Jamshoro Pakistan for access to material characterisation facilities.
The corresponding authors on behalf of all the author state that there is no conflict of interest for this manuscript and that all authors have approved to this version of article for publication.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2019.01.034.
